dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for breathing problems.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been short of breath for a couple of days and called 911 because it's getting worse.
Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Pupils are equal, round, reactive to light. Pupils are noted as pearl.
No signs of cyanosis observed.
No pain reported in the head or neck, and no signs of trauma or rigidity.
Have you felt any pain in your chest, abdomen, or anywhere else?
No pain at all.
Airway is patent, breathing is present but with wheezes, and circulation is presumed intact.
Blood pressure is 158 over 94, pulse 128, respiratory rate 28.
SpO2 is 97%, glucose levels are at 101.
Lung sounds are diminished with inspiratory and expiratory wheezes noted. Patient speaks in two to three word sentences.
Blood pressure is now 136 over 87, pulse 130, respiratory rate 36, and SpO2 at 100%.
IV started on the left arm with 18 gauge. Nebulizer therapy administered with albuterol and Atrovent. Oxygen at 6 LPM via nasal cannula.
Blood pressure is 145 over 95, pulse 108, respiratory rate 18, and SpO2 is 99%.
10 mg Dexamethasone administered via intravenous push.
You mentioned you have been using your nebulizer more frequently. Have you noticed any changes in your symptoms since then?
I note your medical history listed COPD. Are there any other issues you'd like to bring to our attention?
EKG initiated but interpretation is pending due to unclear tracing.
Skin is normal, warm, and dry.
Good peripheral pulses. No edema or trauma noted on extremities or back.
Patient is secured with all straps and positioned for safe transport.
Patient wishes to be transported to Memorial Regional Medical Center. Transport decision is confirmed.
The patient was alert and stable upon arrival at the hospital. He was transferred to the ER bed safely.
All findings and treatments were explained to the ER nurse, and patient care was officially handed over.
No signs of cyanosis or other skin discoloration noted.
No signs of neck masses, lymphadenopathy, or scarring during the head and neck exam.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 now and has remained stable.
Demonstrated appropriate motor response during examination.
No indication of SWM or visual field deficits observed.
No rashes, bites, or birthmarks were palpated or visualized on the skin exam.
Pupils remain reactive bilaterally without abnormal extraocular movement.
No obvious asymmetry in chest expansion or retractions.
Heart sounds are unremarkable.
Current pulse remains within range of signs associated with distress.
Respiratory rate is significantly higher than baseline for this patient.
Glucose initially read as 101 and then again as zero twice - assay device may need validating again.
Patient unimpaired in swallowing during exam.
Abdominal assessment revealed no pain or rigidity, and bowel sounds are present.
Pelvic exam shows absence of tenderness or instability.
No inability to move limbs or report any extremity pain.
Exam of spine and flank shows no signs of bony deformity or bruising.
You've mentioned symptoms starting days ago - what was your last oral intake before calling?
Did you take albuterol today or the day before?
Last meal was dinner yesterday around 7 PM.
You are currently on novolog, metformin, glypizide, diltiazem, and lantus.
No known drug allergies for you originally mentioned.
Tachycardia remains, though somewhat stabilized.
Improved breath sounds following nebulizer therapy have been noted.
No need for advanced airway interventions performed.
No additional medications requested by the patient.
Post treatment, patient reports increased ease with speaking in short phrases.
EKG continued, remains absent of meaningful finding towards acid-base problem.
Patient shows consistent signs of moderate distress, continuing baseline exam and vital signs monitoring.
B/P remains stable with current therapies going forward.
Pulse still elevated yet below original readings - response to fluids and medications ongoing.
Respiration stable at 18, similar overall improvement from before treatments.
Nebulizer evidenced effectiveness by slight increase in short word dialogue.
Monitoring ongoing for any adverse response.
Securing transported equipment as ambient conditions remain stable.
Ensuring ambilocation and transfer during transport is going to be safe and symptom-friendly.
Let's prepare for the ride to the hospital. Everything seems to be working well around here.
Patient vital signs sustained without adverse changes.
Yep, that's fine with me - taking me in now.
We have incorporated all supportive apparatus and readying stretcher inside.
Unit 3 transporting patient, stable, 54 y/o male, COPD, ongoing outpatient evaluation requested.
Patient en route to MRMC, vitals steady, breaths easier.
Final set of vitals taken after positioning - all stable and acceptable to transport.
All findings and medical interventions sent via wire. Final EKG remains non-productive with clear EKG trace absent.
Upward transition setup to hospital doors now complete.
Hospital, this is EMS. We are arriving with a 54 year old male with COPD exacerbation reported. Vitals stable, medications given, and EKG non-diagnostic.
Patient handed over to hospital with documented care plans, forms completed, and manifest secured.
No signs of abnormal skin turgor or signs of widespread dehydration.
No scars on thorax, lungs still show audible wheezing, and no chest pain reported so far.
No signs of abnormal frequency or color in the urinary system with lengthy delay to last meal.
Correlated signs of COPD-related distress per their history and lab use description.
Patient maintains full motor and sensory function with no reported weakness signs.
No signs of changed behavior or unresponsiveness from initial to OOH evaluation.
Palpated neck without any abnormal lumps or stiffness.
No sign of genital involvement or symptoms.
IV line secured and observation of flow without complications reported.
Attempted retake EKG led to similar results - noninitialized.
Signs checked and rechecked - no contradictory emerging findings.
All straps and harness in readiness so as not to intrude or cause positional distress.
All documentation is current, vitals stable - preparation steps complete and patient ready to go.
Appearance of symptoms remains consistent with staging done indoors and after nebulizers.
All signs consistent with staging done indoors and after nebulizers.
Monitors within operation but continuous observation minimized due to stable readings.
No additional procedures or meds to pass through remaining transport sector.
Transport management as per protocol 3-11: Respiratory distress/COPD.
All transcription and materials airborne for hospital system.
Hospital staff receives care plans and all live vitals from monitor.
No issues observed during final turn onto stretcher.
Last checkup in transport shows sustained vitals with no decline or intervention needed.
Paperwork completed and handed to facility with clear verbal update and signatures finalized.
All necessary motions concluded, and ambulance ready for return.
I give signature in consent for treatment and disposition records.
Patient transport is complete with no adverse events from our team. Ready to hand over and go home.
